Title: A Catharanthus roseus Fe(II)/α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase catalyzes a redox-neutral reaction responsible for vindolinine biosynthesis

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Bioactive specialized metabolites; vindolinine-related alkaloids contribute to MIA diversity
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Cyclizes the VNS-generated reactive intermediate to form 19R-vindolinine, 19S-vindolinine, and venalstonine; also catalyzes reverse cycloaddition of tabersonine/vincadifformine to regenerate secodine-type intermediates enabling vindolinine formation.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HL2 is required with VNS to convert dehydrosecodine-derived intermediates into vindolinine epimers and venalstonine. 2. HL2 can catalyze the reverse reaction from tabersonine or vincadifformine to secodine-type intermediates, which are then converted by VNS/HL2 to vindolinines or dihydrovindolinines. 3. HL2 is more active than HL3 in this branch, while HL1 cannot substitute for vindolinine formation.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; in vitro enzyme assays with recombinant proteins; substrate feeding; LC-MS/MS product identification
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro assays with O-acetylprecondylocarpine or reverse reactions from tabersonine/vincadifformine; transient expression in tobacco leaves
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Also referred to as hydrolase 2/tabersonine synthase (HL2/TabS). Functions as a cyclase rather than a transcriptional regulator or redox enzyme. Homologous hydrolases HL4 and VmHL could substitute in vitro, but HL1 could not.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be co-expressed with VNS and upstream secodine-generating enzymes to engineer vindolinine/venalstonine branch production in heterologous systems.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Diverts flux away from tabersonine under VNS-active conditions.

Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Bioactive specialized metabolites; contribute to MIA structural diversity
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supplies the unstable dehydrosecodine intermediate from O-acetylprecondylocarpine, enabling VNS-dependent diversion toward vindolinine and venalstonine biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DPAS-generated dehydrosecodine is the precursor pool used by VNS and HL2 for vindolinine/venalstonine formation. 2. In vitro, addition of VNS to DPAS+HL2 reactions diverts flux from tabersonine to vindolinines and venalstonine. 3. In the absence of HL2, DPAS further reduces VNS-derived intermediates to form 19S/R-14,15-dihydrovindolinines.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; in vitro enzyme assays with recombinant proteins; substrate feeding; LC-MS/MS
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: NADPH-dependent in vitro assays; OAP substrate feeding
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: DPAS is an upstream helper enzyme essential for generating the reactive intermediate but was not the novel discovery focus of the paper.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Needed as part of pathway reconstruction to channel flux into vindolinine biosynthesis.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Can support competing tabersonine/catharanthine branch metabolism depending on partner cyclase.

Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Fe(II)/α-ketoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase (Fe/2OG dioxygenase); contains HXDXnH facial triad and YXnRXS α-ketoglutarate-binding motif
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Monoterpenoid indole alkaloid (MIA) biosynthesis; vindolinine/venalstonine branch from dehydrosecodine
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Alternative cyclization branch diverging from tabersonine-forming dehydrosecodine intermediate
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Dehydrosecodine (generated from O-acetylprecondylocarpine by DPAS or from tabersonine reverse cycloaddition by HL2)
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Reactive radical intermediate leading to 19R-vindolinine, 19S-vindolinine, and venalstonine after HL2/HL4-mediated cyclization
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes a highly unusual redox-neutral isomerization/radical-forming reaction on dehydrosecodine using α-ketoglutarate, O2, and ascorbate, diverting the intermediate toward alternatively cyclized vindolinine epimers and venalstonine rather than tabersonine.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key branch-switch enzyme for vindolinine biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for vindolinine biosynthesis in planta and sufficient, with upstream/downstream partners, to redirect dehydrosecodine flux from tabersonine to 19R-vindolinine, 19S-vindolinine, and venalstonine.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Bioactive specialized alkaloids; related to MIA chemical diversity and medicinal alkaloid scaffold generation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly represented in leaf epidermal transcriptome (EST 83); enriched in leaf epidermis versus whole leaf; also detected in flowers, roots, and stems
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaf epidermis-enriched; present in leaves, flowers, roots, and stems
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Requires α-ketoglutarate and ascorbate in vitro; Fe(II) stimulates activity; D2O causes +1 deuterium incorporation into products
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. VNS was identified from the C. roseus leaf epidermal transcriptome as a unique Fe/2OG dioxygenase candidate for vindolinine biosynthesis. 2. VIGS of VNS reduced VNS transcripts by 93% and lowered 19R-/19S-vindolinine levels by 95% in C. roseus leaves. 3. In tobacco transient expression and in vitro assays, VNS redirected dehydrosecodine flux from tabersonine to 19R-vindolinine, 19S-vindolinine, and venalstonine. 4. VNS performs a rare redox-neutral reaction with respect to the alkaloid substrate/product and depends on α-ketoglutarate and ascorbate. 5. HL2/HL4 or VmHL can cyclize the VNS-derived intermediate, whereas HL1 cannot substitute.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Leaf epidermal transcriptome mining; qRT-PCR; virus-induced gene silencing in C. roseus; transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana; recombinant protein purification; in vitro enzyme assays; D2O labeling; LC-MS/MS; NMR-supported metabolite identification
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Silencing strongly reduces vindolinines and causes small changes in catharanthine and vindoline levels, indicating pathway flux redistribution.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Strong target for metabolic engineering of rare vindolinine and venalstonine scaffolds; should be stacked with DPAS and compatible hydrolases (HL2/HL4) for heterologous reconstruction; C-terminal tagging can abolish activity and should be avoided.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Leaf epidermal transcriptome/EST dataset; raw data/source data provided with paper; no public accession for transcriptome stated
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: VNS is inactive on OAP or tabersonine directly and likely acts on dehydrosecodine. C-terminal His-tagging almost abolished activity. The closest described homolog in Vinca minor shared 48% amino acid identity, and no homolog >50% identity was found in several non-vindolinine-producing MIA species.
